previous action in seating Rev.
Hindley. They claim that he could
do the cause Of labor no possible
harm if granted a seat, but that the
mere fact that he was refused a
seat was one of the hardest blows
dealt the local unions in years, as it
leads the general public to suppose
that the local labor organizations
are dominated by the Retail Liquor
Dealers association.


Apologists for the bartenders
claim the action of the Central La
Dor union was taken on purely con-
stitutional grounds, but this state
ment is not taken seriously by
those familiar with the facts. Min
listers have been seated in the con-
vention of the American Federation
Of Labor and in the State Fedora
ton of Labor, as well as in local
central bodies in other cities.


Union men of this and other
cities believe the clergy as a rule
has opposed labor organization, but
they say that most of this opposi-
ton has been due to ignorance and
this would be remedied by allowing
them to investigate and understand
union principles by actual contact
